does: This paper reveals that Concept Bottleneck Models and Sparse Autoencoders, despite their different training paradigms, both learn the same geometric structure: nonnegative cones spanned by linear directions in activation space representing concepts. The authors formalize this unification by showing that CBMs use supervision to construct these cones while SAEs discover them unsupervised through sparse coding. Building on this geometric insight, they establish a quantitative framework for comparing the two approaches by measuring how well SAE-learned cones approximate or contain CBM-defined cones, enabling evaluation of how different SAE design choices—sparsity levels, expansion ratios, and architecture types—influence the emergence of meaningful concepts that align with human interpretations.
connects: This work bridges two previously disconnected interpretability traditions. CBMs emerged from the need for transparent, human-aligned model reasoning by explicitly supervising activations to match human concepts. SAEs developed from sparse coding literature and representation learning, discovering latent directions without human supervision. Prior work explored concept discovery and disentangled representations separately, but few frameworks unified supervised and unsupervised concept learning. This paper extends recent geometric perspectives on neural representations and builds on foundational work in sparse coding, while responding to growing interest in reconciling interpretability approaches across different mechanistic assumptions about how concepts arise in neural networks.
enables: This geometric framework opens pathways for hybrid interpretability approaches that leverage both paradigms' strengths. The containment metrics provide principled ways to evaluate SAEs against gold-standard human concepts, potentially improving unsupervised concept discovery through this feedback. The cone geometry suggests new regularization techniques that encourage alignment between emergent and prescribed concepts. Future work could develop methods that jointly optimize for both sparsity and human-concept alignment, scale these ideas to larger models, or use the geometric structure to discover which concepts naturally emerge versus require supervision. This unification also enables researchers to systematically study how model architecture, training objectives, and data properties shape the geometry of learned concept spaces.
